target.title: feat(gateway): add LLM request context headers for AI gateway integra…
target.body:
…tion

Propagate session, attempt, and turn-level context via HTTP headers to enable AI gateways to extract tracing/billing information without JWT parsing.

Headers injected:
- X-OpenClaw-Session-Id: session identifier
- X-OpenClaw-Attempt-Id: attempt (run) identifier
- X-OpenClaw-Fallback: "true" when using fallback model after primary failure
- X-OpenClaw-Turn-Index: monotonically increasing turn index per LLM call
- X-OpenClaw-Turn-Id: unique turn identifier ({attemptId}-turn-{index})

The turn-level headers are injected via streamFn wrapper to ensure each HTTP request gets a unique index, since one prompt() call may trigger multiple LLM HTTP requests (one per tool-result round).

Also adds `isFallback` field to PluginHookLlmOutputEvent for plugin visibility into fallback model usage.

target.title: fix(agents): count exec-path openclaw cron add in successfulCronAdds
target.body:
### What

This PR fixes an issue where the `successfulCronAdds` counter was not incremented when an agent ran `openclaw cron add` as a shell command via `exec` or `bash`.

### Why

The existing logic only checked for the structured `cron` tool. This caused the agent runner to misread the counter and incorrectly append "I did not schedule a reminder" to the transcript after a successful shell-based cron addition.

### How

An `else if` block is added to `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` to inspect `exec`/`bash` results. It uses an anchored regex (`/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i`) to identify the command and requires a success receipt (`created|scheduled`) in the output before incrementing the counter.

This approach incorporates the hardened regex requested in the prior (self-closed) PR #53024. New regression tests are included to cover the fix and prevent bypasses.

Closes #52972

target.title: fix: prevent re-entrant loop in internal hook trigger
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes re-entrant infinite loop in `triggerInternalHook` when a handler calls `triggerInternalHook` again with the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination.

Fixes #55211

## Problem

Before this fix, if a hook handler (e.g., `command:new`) triggered the same hook again internally, it would cause an infinite loop because there was no active-dispatch guard.

## Solution

Added an `AsyncLocalStorage`-based re-entrancy guard:
- Re-entrant calls within the same async call chain are blocked (handler → triggerInternalHook → same key → sees inherited store → dropped)
- Independent concurrent calls from different async contexts are NOT blocked (preserves at-least-once delivery)
- Delayed same-key hooks scheduled by handlers (e.g. via setTimeout fire-and-forget) are delivered after dispatch completes, because the guard key is cleared from the Set in a `finally` block

## Changes
- `src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`: Added `AsyncLocalStorage` guard with `try/finally` guard-key cleanup around `triggerInternalHook`
- `src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`: Added 34 lines of tests covering recursive, sequential, concurrent, cross-session, and delayed redispatch behavior

## Compatibility contract

Same-chain same-key suppression is the intended internal hook contract. This prevents infinite re-entrant loops while preserving:
- Independent concurrent dispatches (fireAndForgetHook pattern)
- Delayed follow-up hooks scheduled from handlers
- Cross-session and cross-event-type independence

Plugins or hook packs that rely on nested same-key hook delivery within the same async chain will see those calls suppressed. This is the correct behavior to prevent infinite amplification.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Re-entrant `triggerInternalHook` infinite loop (same `type:action:sessionKey` triggered within its own handler chain). Additionally: guard key lifetime — the guard must be cleared after dispatch so delayed same-key hooks are not blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x64, Node v24.14.0. Standalone reproduction script (`repro-guard-lifetime.mjs`) mirroring the exact guard logic from this PR, run directly with `node` — no monorepo build or test runner required.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node repro-guard-lifetime.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:** Standalone reproduction script output showing 5 scenarios:

```
Real Behavior Proof: AsyncLocalStorage Guard Lifetime
Date: 2026-05-23T11:48:53.225Z
Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64

Scenario 1: Re-entrant call during dispatch → BLOCKED
  Handler called: 1 time(s)
  PASS: re-entrant calls blocked during dispatch

Scenario 2: Sequential triggers after first completes → DELIVERED
  Handler called: 3 time(s)
  PASS: sequential triggers all delivered

Scenario 3: Concurrent independent triggers → BOTH DELIVERED
  Handler called: 2 time(s)
  PASS: concurrent independent triggers both delivered

Scenario 4: Delayed same-key hook AFTER dispatch completes → DELIVERED
  After dispatch: handler called 1 time(s)
  After 50ms wait: handler called 2 time(s)
  Delayed trigger delivered: true
  PASS: delayed same-key hook delivered after dispatch completes

Scenario 5: Different session keys → independent
  Handler called: 2 time(s)
  PASS: different session keys are independent

SUMMARY
  Re-entrant during dispatch  → blocked (1 call)      PASS
  Sequential after complete   → all delivered (3)      PASS
  Concurrent independent      → both delivered (2)     PASS
  Delayed after complete      → delivered (2 calls)    PASS
  Different session keys      → independent (2)        PASS

  The guard key is cleared from the AsyncLocalStorage Set
  after dispatch completes (via try/finally). Delayed
  same-key hooks that fire after dispatch return are
  correctly delivered, not blocked by the stale guard.
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 5 scenarios pass. Re-entrant calls are blocked during dispatch (Scenario 1). Sequential (Scenario 2), concurrent (Scenario 3), and delayed (Scenario 4) triggers are all delivered. Different session keys are independent (Scenario 5). The guard key cleanup in `finally` ensures delayed same-key hooks scheduled from handlers are not blocked after the original dispatch returns.

**What was not tested:**
- Multi-process concurrent triggers (single-process only)
- Performance impact under extreme load (normal usage validated)
- Windows path handling (not path-dependent)

target.title: fix(secrets): allow hash in exec SecretRef ids
target.body:
## Summary
- allow `#` in exec SecretRef ids while preserving traversal-segment rejection
- add a shared valid test vector for the documented AWS Secrets Manager `secret#json_key` convention
- keep config, plan, gateway protocol, and plugin SDK validation in parity
- sync the public configuration reference with the broadened exec SecretRef id pattern

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** exec SecretRef ids that include an AWS-style JSON-key selector such as `gravity/shared/slack-gateway/credentials#bot_token` should pass OpenClaw config validation and reach the exec provider path instead of being rejected by the SecretRef id contract.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw CLI on macOS from branch `fix/secretref-exec-hash-ids`, with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` pointed at isolated `/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof` files so no user gateway state was touched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/80712-openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/state-80712 pnpm openclaw config validate --json`
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the real CLI run:

  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/80712-openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/state-80712 pnpm openclaw config validate --json
  $ node scripts/run-node.mjs config validate --json
  {"valid":true,"path":"/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-proof/80712-openclaw.json"}
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The config containing `models.providers.openai.apiKey.id = "gravity/shared/slack-gateway/credentials#bot_token"` returned `valid: true`, confirming the real OpenClaw config validation path now accepts `#json_key` exec SecretRef ids.
- **Not tested:** Did not call a live AWS Secrets Manager resolver; this proof verifies the OpenClaw validation/gateway config path accepts the documented exec SecretRef id so the configured provider can resolve it at runtime.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/secrets/ref-contract.test.ts src/secrets/exec-secret-ref-id-parity.test.ts src/config/config.secrets-schema.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/primitives.secretref.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/secret-input.test.ts src/secrets/plan.test.ts src/secrets/resolve.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md src/secrets/ref-contract.ts src/secrets/ref-contract.test.ts src/secrets/exec-secret-ref-id-parity.test.ts src/test-utils/secret-ref-test-vectors.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Fixes #80712

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

commands.review_followup: A narrow automated repair can preserve the validated CLI bug fix while removing the v4 wire-schema compatibility break.

target.title: fix(cli): apply sibling_ref SecretRef assignments via setPathCreateStrict
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: `openclaw status` and `openclaw channels list` (any CLI command going through `resolveCommandSecretRefsViaGateway`) crashes when a channel uses a `sibling_ref`-shape SecretRef and the user's config is in the legacy ref-only state — today this is `@openclaw/googlechat` with `serviceAccountRef` configured but no sibling `serviceAccount`. The resolved-value path is intentionally absent; the CLI's `setPathExistingStrict` rejects writing it.
- **Why it matters**: blocks routine inspection commands for any user with `sibling_ref` channels in legacy ref-only shape. Currently this is googlechat; the contract is generic.
- **What changed**: extends `secrets.resolve` gateway protocol with a per-assignment `createIfMissing: boolean`. Gateway-side serializer populates it from `target.entry.secretShape === "sibling_ref"`. CLI apply branches on the flag at the three apply sites (main loop + local resolution + re-apply after fallback). Sibling-ref assignments use `setPathCreateStrict`; `secret_input` shapes keep `setPathExistingStrict` so a typo'd path still surfaces as an error.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: runtime apply path (`src/secrets/apply.ts`) was already correct — it uses `setPathCreateStrict` for `sibling_ref` shapes. The CLI apply was the outlier; this PR aligns it. No changes to googlechat plugin or its `secret-contract.ts`. No changes to which channel is sibling_ref vs secret_input.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: PR #76449 (the recent secrets-resolve external-channel contracts work — same problem family on the gateway-runtime side; this PR extends the alignment to the CLI apply side).
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `openclaw status`, `openclaw channels list`, and `openclaw channels status --probe` previously crashed with `Error: failed to apply resolved secret assignment at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount (Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount.)` for any user whose googlechat config uses the legacy `serviceAccountRef`-only shape. After this fix, those commands exit 0.

- **Real environment tested**: Raspberry Pi 5 (`hostname: ultron`, `aarch64`), Linux `6.12.75+rpt-rpi-2712`, Node `22.22.2`, with prod state at `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` containing `channels.googlechat.serviceAccountRef` (a `SecretRef` resolving via a `file:` provider to a Google Cloud service-account JSON). Branch `fix/cli-secret-apply-sibling-ref` rebased against current `upstream/main`:
  - Commit: `f8f931c810a` (rebased against upstream/main `99b17263a12` on 2026-05-06)
  - Tarball: `openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz`, sha256 `caf526329a7aa166569673460c023dd21564ea4ca5135c58d47e834098ebe847`
  - Installed globally via `npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz` → `OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (f8f931c)`
  - `@openclaw/googlechat` plugin: `2026.5.2-beta.2` (the version Eric has in production for this regression).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # In the rebased fix-branch worktree:
  pnpm install && pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts

  # Replace global install with the rebased build:
  systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway.service
  npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz
  openclaw --version    # → OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (f8f931c)
  systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway.service
  # wait for port 18789 to listen

  # Run the three previously-crashing CLI commands against prod state:
  openclaw status; echo "exit=$?"
  openclaw channels list; echo "exit=$?"
  openclaw channels status --probe; echo "exit=$?"

  # Verify the bug-A error is absent:
  grep -c 'Path segment does not exist' <captured outputs>
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  All three commands exit 0 against prod state (full captures in `./artifacts/smoke-bug-a-fix-on-pi-rebased/`):
  ```
  TEST 1: openclaw status                  exit=0
  TEST 2: openclaw channels list           exit=0
  TEST 3: openclaw channels status --probe exit=0

  bug-A markers (Path segment does not exist) — must be 0 across all three captures:
    test-1-status.out                : 0
    test-2-channels-list.out         : 0
    test-3-channels-probe.out        : 0
  ```

  Targeted regression tests (mocking `callGateway` to return assignments with and without the new `createIfMissing` flag):
  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  25 passed (25)
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 22.58s
  ```

  Two new test cases verify the gate-and-fallback behavior:
  - `creates the missing leaf when assignment marks createIfMissing (sibling_ref shape)` — gateway returns assignment with `createIfMissing: true`, missing leaf, succeeds, value is written under the new leaf while existing sibling ref is preserved.
  - `still throws when assignment lacks createIfMissing and the path does not exist` — backward-compatibility: an older gateway omits the flag, CLI keeps using `setPathExistingStrict`, mis-addressed assignments still error.

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - `openclaw status` exits 0; status table renders cleanly with Google Chat present.
  - `openclaw channels list` exits 0 and includes Google Chat.
  - `openclaw channels status --probe` exits 0 and probes Google Chat as configured.
  - The pre-fix `Error: Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount` is gone from all three captures.
  - Backward-compatible: assignments without `createIfMissing` keep the existing `setPathExistingStrict` semantics.

- **What was not tested**: End-to-end outbound message delivery via the runtime path (intentionally separate from the CLI inspection path this PR fixes; runtime path was already correct and was not modified here).


### Pi 5 timings

CLI commands previously crashing with `Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount` now exit 0 against a SecretRef-backed prod config. Wall-clock measured with `time(1)` on the host:

| Step | real | notes |
|---|---|---|
| `time openclaw status` | **15.2 s** | post-fix, gateway up; renders channel table including googlechat row. |
| `time openclaw channels list` | **13.6 s** | post-fix, gateway up. |
| `time openclaw gateway restart` | **38.6 s** | defensive: gateway boot path remains healthy under systemd. |
| `time pnpm test src/cli/command-secret-gateway.test.ts` | **23.9 s** | 25/25 pass — includes the two new tests in this PR. |

Build pipeline wall-clock on the same host (incremental rebuild from edited source, not a fresh-checkout cold build — `pnpm install` + node_modules cache retained):

| Step | real |
|---|---|
| `time pnpm build` | 36.3 s |
| `time pnpm ui:build` | 4.4 s |
| `time npm pack --ignore-scripts` | 18.1 s |
| `time npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz` | 44.3 s (includes per-arch native-module rebuild) |

Pi-class hardware notes: this is the same Raspberry Pi 5 (8 GiB) class previously surfaced in PR #76227's timing data. The 15-second `openclaw status` floor is dominated by ~13 seconds of plugin-loader + gateway WebSocket round-trip; the secret-resolve apply step that this PR fixes adds no measurable overhead on top.

- **What was not tested**:
  - End-to-end outbound message delivery to a Google Chat space — that's the runtime path, which is intentionally separate from the CLI inspection path this PR fixes. The runtime path was already correct (`src/secrets/apply.ts` uses `setPathCreateStrict` for sibling_ref shapes).
  - Other potential `sibling_ref` channels — googlechat is the only externalized channel currently using `secretShape: "sibling_ref"`; the contract is generic but no other consumers exist on `main` today.

- **Before evidence (optional)**:

  Reproduction on the same Pi 5 prior to applying the fix (captured 2026-05-03):
  ```
  $ openclaw channels list
  ...
  [openclaw] Failed to start CLI: Error: channels: failed to apply resolved
    secret assignment at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount
    (Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount.).
      at resolveCommandSecretRefsViaGateway
        (src/cli/command-secret-gateway.ts:751)
  ```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: gateway resolves the SecretRef and returns an assignment whose `pathSegments` target the *resolved-value* path (a sibling of the SecretRef path). For `sibling_ref` shapes, that target path is intentionally absent from the user's source config when they're on the legacy ref-only shape. The CLI apply unconditionally used `setPathExistingStrict`, which throws if any segment in the chain is missing.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: the `secrets.resolve` protocol payload didn't carry the per-assignment shape hint, and the CLI couldn't distinguish "user typo'd a path" from "this is a sibling_ref shape and the resolved-value path is intentionally absent".
- **Contributing context**: the runtime apply path in `src/secrets/apply.ts:335` and `:564` already handles this correctly (it uses `setPathCreateStrict` for sibling_ref shapes). The CLI apply was the outlier. PR #76449 brought the runtime side in line with external channel contracts; this PR extends the alignment to the CLI inspection side.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- **Target test or file**: `src/cli/command-secret-gateway.test.ts` (extended) and `src/secrets/command-config.test.ts` (already covers gateway-side serialization).
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: gateway-returned assignment with `createIfMissing: true` and a missing leaf — apply must succeed and write the value under the new leaf while preserving the existing sibling SecretRef. Conversely: assignment without `createIfMissing` and a missing leaf must continue to throw.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: tests cover the wire-protocol round-trip (mocked `callGateway`) plus the apply-loop branch on `createIfMissing`, which is exactly where the original bug fired.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: `command-config.test.ts` covers the gateway-side serialization of `secretShape: "sibling_ref"` → `createIfMissing: true`. The two new CLI-side tests in `command-secret-gateway.test.ts` lock in the apply-side branching.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw status`, `openclaw channels list`, `openclaw channels status --probe`, and any other CLI command going through `resolveCommandSecretRefsViaGateway` no longer crash for users with `sibling_ref`-shape SecretRef channels in legacy ref-only configuration. No defaults or config keys changed.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
gateway.secrets.resolve({ ... })
  -> assignments: [{ pathSegments: ['channels','googlechat','serviceAccount'], value: {...} }]
  -> CLI apply: setPathExistingStrict(resolvedConfig, pathSegments, value)
  -> THROWS: "Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount."

After:
gateway.secrets.resolve({ ... })
  -> assignments: [{ pathSegments: [...], value: {...}, createIfMissing: true }]
                                                       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
                                                       new field, set when
                                                       target.entry.secretShape === "sibling_ref"
  -> CLI apply: assignment.createIfMissing
                  ? setPathCreateStrict(resolvedConfig, pathSegments, value)
                  : setPathExistingStrict(resolvedConfig, pathSegments, value)
  -> SUCCEEDS: missing leaf created with the resolved value; existing
               sibling ref preserved unchanged.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` (the change is in how the resolved value is written into the in-memory config; the resolution itself is unchanged)
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux `6.12.75+rpt-rpi-2712` (Raspberry Pi 5, aarch64)
- Runtime/container: Node `22.22.2`, openclaw managed-systemd gateway
- Model/provider: N/A (CLI inspection path; no model interaction)
- Integration/channel: `@openclaw/googlechat 2026.5.2-beta.2` with `serviceAccountRef` SecretRef pointing at a `file:` provider
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "googlechat": {
        "serviceAccountRef": {
          "source": "file",
          "provider": "googlechat-sa",
          "id": "value"
        }
      }
    },
    "secrets": {
      "providers": {
        "googlechat-sa": { "path": "<redacted absolute path to service-account JSON>" }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Stop systemd gateway: `systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway.service`.
2. Install fix-branch build: `npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz` (sha256 `caf526329a7a…`).
3. Start gateway: `systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway.service`; wait for port 18789 to listen.
4. Run: `openclaw status`, `openclaw channels list`, `openclaw channels status --probe`.

### Expected

- All three commands exit 0; Google Chat appears as a configured channel.

### Actual

- All three commands exit 0; `bug-A markers ('Path segment does not exist') == 0` across all three captures.

## Evidence

Attached:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — full captures at `./artifacts/smoke-bug-a-fix-on-pi-rebased/` and pre-fix capture at `./artifacts/smoke-bug-a-fix-on-pi/` (older but same shape — same Pi, same prod state).
- [x] Trace/log snippets — pre-fix `Path segment does not exist at channels.googlechat.serviceAccount` stack quoted above; post-fix exit 0.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — N/A (CLI-only path).
- [ ] Perf numbers — N/A.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: post-fix `openclaw status`, `openclaw channels list`, `openclaw channels status --probe` against my prod state with `sibling_ref`-shape googlechat config; targeted regression tests (`pnpm test src/cli/command-secret-gateway.test.ts src/secrets/command-config.test.ts` → 25/25 pass).
- **Edge cases checked**: backward-compat — assignments without `createIfMissing` (older gateway) still trigger `setPathExistingStrict`; mis-addressed assignments (truly missing target) still throw with the existing error message.
- **What I did NOT verify**: end-to-end outbound message delivery via the runtime path (intentionally separate from the CLI inspection path this PR fixes; runtime path was already correct).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — older gateways omit `createIfMissing` and the CLI defaults to `setPathExistingStrict`. New CLI talking to old gateway: existing behavior preserved. New gateway talking to old CLI: new field is ignored; old CLI will still throw on sibling_ref leaves (no regression vs current behavior).
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: `secret_input`-shape assignments accidentally get `createIfMissing: true` and silently create missing config paths instead of erroring.
  - **Mitigation**: gateway serializer at `src/secrets/command-config.ts` only sets `createIfMissing: true` when `target.entry.secretShape === "sibling_ref"` — explicit shape gate, covered by a regression test.
- **Risk**: Re-apply-after-incomplete-snapshot path could lose the `createIfMissing` flag during recovery.
  - **Mitigation**: extended `UnresolvedCommandSecretAssignment` to also carry `createIfMissing`, propagated through the analyzed.unresolved → re-apply path. Locked in by the apply-loop tests.

target.title: Add ephemeral Control UI Activity tab
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds a Control UI Activity tab at `/activity` under the Control nav group.
- Derives browser-local activity entries from the existing `session.tool` / tool-event path without adding a new Gateway event family, SSE endpoint, protocol version, TUI pane, observer stream, verbose setting, or durable store.
- Stores only sanitized summaries, hidden-argument counts, and redacted/truncated output previews in Activity state; raw tool arguments and raw results stay out of the Activity model.
- Adds filtering, tool-name selection, Clear, Expand all, Collapse all, keyboard-native disclosure rows, auto-follow scrolling, navigation, i18n bundles, docs, changelog, and focused tests.

Fixes #12831.

Follow-ups intentionally left open as distinct scope: #54577 timeline CLI/export, #37816 Telegram durable activity, and #47386 WebChat summary mode.

## Verification
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/navigation-groups.test.ts ui/src/ui/navigation.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-scroll.test.ts`
- Browser smoke against Vite Control UI plus a local fake Gateway WebSocket emitting a live `session.tool` event; verified the Activity tab rendered one event, argument values stayed hidden, and output showed redacted/truncated preview text without the raw command, token, or path.
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` (`tbx_01ksd3qkr8ydn75jfttp6hvkpd`, https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26362948981)

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Control UI now exposes an ephemeral Activity tab that observes live tool activity from the existing `session.tool` / tool-event delivery while keeping Chat tool-call hiding independent from the sanitized Activity summaries.

Real environment tested: Local Vite Control UI in a browser connected to a local fake Gateway WebSocket that emitted the client `session.tool` delivery shape; Blacksmith Testbox ran the repo changed-check lane on the rebased pushed diff.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started `pnpm --dir ui dev`, connected `/activity?gatewayUrl=ws://127.0.0.1:18889#token=smoke`, emitted a live `session.tool` event with argument fields plus sensitive-looking output, exercised the Activity filters/disclosure controls/scroll area, ran the focused Vitest commands above, regenerated i18n, ran `git diff --check`, and ran Testbox `pnpm check:changed`.

Evidence after fix: The Activity tab displayed the live tool event as a sanitized row with hidden argument count and redacted preview (`Authorization: [redacted] file=[redacted path] ok`); no raw command, token, absolute path, args JSON, or full result was visible in the Activity UI or stored in Activity state by the new model tests.

Observed result after fix: Activity survives in-app navigation through browser memory, resets on session switch and reload by design, supports Clear, filtering, expand/collapse, and scroll auto-follow, and leaves #54577, #37816, and #47386 as separate follow-up tracks.

What was not tested: A real production Gateway session with real user tool calls was not used; the browser smoke used a local fake Gateway event with the same Control UI `session.tool` delivery shape.

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

- Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted):

```json
{
  "head": "b23d1d7c",
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>",
  "inlineCustomProvider": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "discoverModelsFindsCustomModel": "custom-model"
  },
  "legacyModelsJsonOnlyUpgrade": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsLegacyModel": "legacy-model"
  },
  "catalogOnlyLegacyCleanup": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsCatalogOnlyModel": "catalog-only-model"
  },
  "envPresentCatalogMigration": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": false,
      "keyRef": {
        "source": "env",
        "provider": "default",
        "id": "LITELLM_KEY"
      },
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "apiKeyMarker": "LITELLM_KEY",
      "resolvedEnvValueSeen": true
    }
  },
  "ambiguousEnvShapedCredential": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {

      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",

    }

}
```

- Observed result after fix: the final `models.json` omits plaintext keys; non-built-in custom providers with inline model rows retain the non-secret `secretref-managed` marker; old configured and catalog-only `models.json` credentials migrate into `auth-profiles.json`; env-present legacy catalog values migrate as env-backed `keyRef` credentials and resolve through provider auth; ambiguous env-shaped legacy values with no env signal are preserved as inline local auth-profile keys instead of unresolved env refs; a non-main agent with an inherited same-profile ID still migrates its local legacy catalog key and runtime auth resolves the worker-local key; and pi `discoverModels` can still find custom model rows.
- What was not tested: I did not test a real paid third-party provider. The live proof did not force auth-profile persistence failure; that fail-closed path is covered by the focused regression test listed below.

target.title: fix(agents): log warnings instead of swallowing subagent errors
target.body:
## Problem

Two catch blocks in the subagent registry silently discard errors, leaving no diagnostic trail when hook emission or disk restore fails:

1. **`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`** (`subagent-registry-completion.ts:116`): `catch { return false; }` discards hook-runner errors when emitting the `subagent_ended` lifecycle hook.
2. **`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`** (`subagent-registry.ts:645`): `catch { // ignore restore failures }` discards errors when restoring subagent runs from disk on cold start.

When either path fails (hook runner unavailable, corrupt disk state, reconciliation error), the system continues silently. Operators have no indication that subagent lifecycle hooks were skipped or that restored runs were lost.

## Fix

Wire `log.warn()` into both catch blocks so errors are logged before the existing fallback behavior continues:

1. **Hook emission path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ..."); return false; }`
2. **Restore path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ..."); }`

Both paths still return/continue as before (no behavior change beyond logging). The completion file gets its own `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` to match the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")` in the registry file.

Production diff is +8 lines changed across 2 files.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Two silent `catch` blocks in the subagent registry discard errors during hook emission and disk restore. The fix adds `log.warn()` to both paths so failures leave a diagnostic trail instead of being silently swallowed.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-82943`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82943
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify both log.warn calls are in compiled production code
grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js

Step 3. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

Step 4. Run test suites for both changed files
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

Both `log.warn` calls are present in the compiled subagent-registry bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
94:log$2.warn(`failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);

$ grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
1911:log.warn(`failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);
```

Line 94 is `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` (the hook emission path). Line 1911 is `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (the disk restore path). Before this fix, both were `catch { }` (empty) and `catch { // ignore restore failures }` respectively.

The `log$2` prefix on line 94 is the compiled form of the new `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")`, while `log` on line 1911 is the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")`.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The patched gateway starts, loads the subagent registry with both warning-enabled catch blocks, and runs cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T12:39:28.862-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T12:39:28.917-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T12:39:33.527-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.421-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.422-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 5.5s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.913-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T12:39:52.803-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T12:39:52.807-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T12:39:52.856-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

No `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` or `[agents/subagent-registry]` warnings appear in the startup log, confirming the catch blocks are not triggered during normal operation (they are defensive logging for error paths only).

**Vitest test suites (supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 86ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 73ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  16 passed (16)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2679ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2264ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  58 passed (58)
```

**Fault injection proof: restore catch path fires in real gateway**

The `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` catch handler was triggered in a **live running gateway** by injecting `throw new Error("INJECTED: corrupt registry state")` at the top of the try block in the compiled `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js`. This forces the catch path to fire during gateway startup.

**Steps:**
1. Back up the compiled subagent-registry bundle.
2. Inject `throw new Error(...)` inside the try block of `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (after `restoreAttempted = true`, before `restoreSubagentRunsFromDisk`).
3. Start the gateway.
4. Observe the `log.warn` message in the gateway output.
5. Confirm the gateway continues to start normally (the catch swallows the error).
6. Restore the original bundle.

**Terminal output (fault injection):**

```console
2026-05-21T16:15:54.095-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T16:15:54.710-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state
2026-05-21T16:15:54.832-07:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
2026-05-21T16:15:56.662-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T16:15:56.665-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 2.6s)
2026-05-21T16:15:57.476-07:00 [gateway] ready
```

**Line-by-line analysis:**

- `[gateway] starting...`: Gateway startup begins.
- `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`: The **`log.warn` in the catch handler fired**. The error message includes the full error text. Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { // ignore restore failures }` with no logging at all.
- `[gateway] ready`: The gateway **continued to start normally** despite the restore failure. The catch handler swallowed the error and let startup proceed.

Without this fix, the same error would be silently ignored (empty catch block with only a comment), leaving no diagnostic trail.

**Fault injection proof: hook emission catch path fires in real gateway**

The `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` catch handler was also triggered in a **live running gateway**. A conditional `throw` was injected inside the try block of `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` in `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js` (line 73), and a timed trigger called the function after the gateway was ready.

**Terminal output (hook emission catch):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:35:44.608-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:35:58.683-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable
2026-05-21T21:35:58.686-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T21:35:58.734-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:35:58.736-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

**Analysis:**
- `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` subsystem prefix confirms the **new** `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` is active (this logger was added by this PR).
- `failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable` is the exact `log.warn` message from the catch block at compiled line 94.
- The gateway continued running after the hook emission failure (the catch returns `false`, not rethrow).
- Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { return false; }` with no logging at all.

**Both catch blocks are now proven in live gateway runs:**
1. **Restore catch** (`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`
2. **Hook emission catch** (`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable`

Observed result after fix: Both new `log.warn` calls fire correctly in production compiled code. The restore catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry]` and the hook emission catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]`. Both allow the gateway to continue running. All 74 tests pass across both test files.

What was not tested: The hook emission was triggered via injected timer call rather than a real subagent completion event. The injection proves the catch handler mechanism (log.warn + return false + gateway continues) works identically to the restore path proof.

target.title: fix(doctor): recover sibling moltbot config
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #54200 by adding a narrow recovery path for the upgrade layout where `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` is missing or skeletal while the user's real legacy config still exists at `~/.openclaw/moltbot.json`.

- `doctor` preflight copies sibling `moltbot.json` into canonical `openclaw.json` when the canonical file is missing.
- If canonical `openclaw.json` exists but is skeletal, read-only doctor now only warns; `openclaw doctor --fix` backs it up and restores from sibling `moltbot.json`.
- The replacement path copies through a temp file, restores the original canonical config on failure, and chmods the recovered canonical config to `0600`.
- Gateway startup adds a targeted hint when `gateway.mode` is missing and the sibling legacy config exists.

This does **not** add `moltbot.json` back to the normal config resolver or public config candidate list.

## Verification

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts --reporter=dot` (2 files, 35 tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/paths.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts --reporter=dot` (4 files, 92 tests passed)
- `git diff --check refs/remotes/origin/main...HEAD`

`pnpm check:changed` reaches an unrelated current-main core-test typecheck failure in `scripts/control-ui-i18n.ts` for missing declarations on `./npm-runner.mjs`, `./pnpm-runner.mjs`, and `./windows-cmd-helpers.mjs`. The core production typecheck lane passed before that failure.

## Real behavior proof

Real temp home: `/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV` with:

- skeletal canonical config: `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` containing only `update.channel`
- sibling legacy config: `~/.openclaw/moltbot.json` containing `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.port=19091`, and `agents.defaults.model`
- legacy file mode before repair: `0644`

Commands run from this branch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --no-workspace-suggestions --non-interactive
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --no-workspace-suggestions --non-interactive --fix
```

Observed after read-only doctor:

- Output included: `Found legacy sibling config at /tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/moltbot.json; run openclaw doctor --fix to recover it into /tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/openclaw.json.`
- `openclaw.json` remained the original skeletal file.
- No `openclaw.json.pre-moltbot-migration.*` backup was created by read-only doctor.

Observed after `doctor --fix`:

- Output included `Backed up skeletal config` and `Migrated legacy config` for `moltbot.json -> openclaw.json`.
- `openclaw.json` contained recovered `gateway.mode: "local"`, `gateway.port: 19091`, and `agents.defaults.model: "openai/gpt-5.5"`.
- Backup existed: `openclaw.json.pre-moltbot-migration.2026-05-24T13-43-20-209Z`.
- Final canonical config permissions: `mode=600 path=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

target.title: fix(webchat): stabilize live transcript run state
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #83528 and #82611; refs #83949.

- Mirror Codex app-server inbound prompts at turn start with the existing `${turnId}:prompt` identity so WebChat can see external-channel user messages before the turn finishes.
- Keep hidden external-channel live runs private by sending chat/tool/agent updates only to exact selected-session message subscribers instead of broadcasting globally.
- Subscribe Control UI to the selected session message stream on connect/reconnect and session switch, adopt observed run ids for non-WebChat-originated runs including the default main alias/canonical key pair, dedupe accumulated stream snapshots around tool cards, and keep selected-session `session.message` reloads alias-aware.
- Emit stable `messageSeq` values for multi-message Codex mirror batches so selected-session subscribers receive per-message transcript positions instead of post-batch duplicates.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: WebChat/Control UI now has durable inbound prompt truth at Codex turn start, selected-session live updates for external-channel runs without unrelated-session leakage, passive run-id adoption and `session.message` handling across the default main alias/canonical key pair, reconnect-safe selected-session subscription state, stable mirrored transcript message sequences, and no duplicated accumulated stream text around tool cards.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw Codex worktree on branch `meow/webchat-transcript-run-state-truth`; behavior proof was rerun after rebasing onto current `origin/main`, on latest head `33df7682a5`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway-chat-load.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
  - `node --import tsx scripts/check-no-extension-test-core-imports.ts`
  - `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Fresh red/green regression shard: 2 files passed, 36 tests passed after failing on missing alias reload and missing `messageSeq`.
  - Codex app-server tests: 2 files passed, 232 tests passed.
  - Gateway/session event tests: 4 files passed, 111 tests passed.
  - Control UI tests: 7 files passed, 238 tests passed.
  - Rebased-base test-type command passed.
  - Boundary check passed: `check-no-extension-test-core-imports` checked 1915 extension files and 1 plugin helper.
  - `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` passed on latest head.
  - The regressions prove message-bearing prompt transcript updates with message ids and stable sequences, multi-message mirror batch `messageSeq` `[1, 2]`, selected-session sequence delivery, alias/canonical subscription de-dupe, stale subscription completion cleanup, main-alias `session.message` reloads, and passive main-alias run-id adoption.
- Observed result after fix: The early Codex prompt mirror writes one user transcript row and emits a message-bearing selected-session update before assistant completion, final mirrored batches emit correct per-message sequence metadata, hidden external-channel chat events route only to exact selected-session subscribers, selected Control UI sessions subscribe/re-subscribe without alias churn or stale async overwrite, canonical `agent:main:main` transcript events reload selected `main`, passive chat state adopts observed run ids, and accumulated stream snapshots render only their new suffix after tool cards.
- What was not tested: Live Feishu or Telegram external-channel browser proof was not run in this pass. Remote `pnpm check:changed` through Crabbox was attempted before this follow-up but blocked: Blacksmith Testbox is not configured for `.github/workflows/crabbox-hydrate.yml`, and default AWS Crabbox could not refresh credentials because no EC2 IMDS role/credentials were available on this machine.

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway-chat-load.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts`

target.title: fix(usage-cost): surface unpriced-model spend as missingCostEntries instead of a confident $0
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw gateway usage-cost` reports `totalCost: 0` with `missingCostEntries: 0` for models that ship an all-zero `cost` block (e.g. codex `gpt-5.5`, whose Codex backend exposes no per-token price). Real pay-per-token usage is therefore reported as a confident, *complete* `$0`, and every budget / spike / daily-spend safeguard that keys off `totalCost` is silently blind to it.
- **Solution:** In `scanTranscriptFile`, when a model's resolved pricing is *unknown* — no positive per-token rate and no tiered pricing — and the turn actually burned tokens, drop the transport's fabricated `$0` and surface the turn as a missing-cost entry instead of recording a confident `$0`.
- **What changed:** `src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts` — new `isModelPricingKnown()` helper and a sibling branch in `scanTranscriptFile` that mirrors the existing tiered-pricing override. `src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts` — regression test.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Models with positive or tiered pricing are unaffected; **operator-configured zero-cost models keep reporting a complete `$0`** (intentional free pricing is preserved); entries with zero token usage are not flagged; a transport-recorded *positive* cost is preserved; recorded costs for priced models are untouched. No change to model-catalog generation or the gateway pricing cache.

## Motivation

A freshly-onboarded, idle host on `2026.5.20` (default `openai/gpt-5.5`, `api_key` auth) accrued ~1.78M tokens/day, yet `usage-cost` reported `$0` with `missingCostEntries: 0`, so budget/spike monitoring never fired. Surfacing unknown-priced usage as *missing* lets downstream tooling tell "genuinely free" apart from "price unknown" instead of trusting a false `$0`. Tracks #85858.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #85858
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `usage-cost` reports a confident `$0` (`missingCostEntries: 0`) for unpriced `gpt-5.5` usage, hiding real spend from budget/spike monitoring.
- Real environment tested: self-hosted OpenClaw `2026.5.20 (e510042)` on Linux; default model `openai/gpt-5.5`; `api_key` auth.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: applied the equivalent change to the installed build, restarted the gateway, then ran `openclaw gateway usage-cost --days 2 --json --expect-final` over the same already-logged usage.
- Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output of the `openclaw gateway usage-cost` command — before vs. after the patch, same logs:

```
BEFORE (shipped build) -- openclaw gateway usage-cost --days 2 --json
  totals: { totalTokens: 1780235, totalCost: 0, missingCostEntries: 0 }

AFTER (patched build) -- same command, same already-logged usage
  totals: { totalTokens: 1780235, totalCost: 0, missingCostEntries: 32 }
```

- Observed result after fix: the same 1,780,235 tokens of otherwise-invisible unpriced usage are now surfaced as 32 missing-cost entries; `totalCost` stays `0` (no fabricated price is invented).
- What was not tested: models configured with *intentional* zero pricing (these would also be surfaced as missing — see scope note below); the macOS/Control-UI cost views.
- Before evidence: included in the terminal capture above.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** codex models ship `cost: {input:0, output:0, cacheRead:0, cacheWrite:0}` in the generated `models.json` because the Codex backend exposes no per-token price. `resolveModelCostConfig` returns that *defined* zero, `estimateUsageCost` computes a confident `0`, and the transport records `cost.total: 0` per turn. Cost aggregation (`applyCostTotal` / `applyCostBreakdown`) only treats `undefined` — not `0` — as missing, so "price unknown" is indistinguishable from "genuinely free."

## Note on the free-vs-unknown distinction

This fix is **source-aware** so it does not break intentionally-free pricing. A turn's all-zero cost is treated as *unknown* (counted toward `missingCostEntries`) only when **all** of these hold: the resolved pricing has no positive/tiered rate, the operator did **not** explicitly configure the model's price under `models.providers` (so the zero is a generated-catalog default, e.g. codex/gpt-5.x), and there is no trustworthy recorded cost (recorded cost is `0`/absent), and the turn actually burned tokens. A model the operator **deliberately** priced at `0`, or any turn carrying a real positive recorded cost, keeps reporting a complete `$0`. (Helper `resolveConfiguredModelCost()` reads config-only pricing to make this distinction; both paths have regression tests.)

Residual nuance for maintainers: this preserves *operator-configured* zero prices, but a non-codex provider whose models are emitted into the generated catalog with literal zeros would still be treated as unknown. Fully distinguishing that case wants an explicit pricing contract (e.g. `cost: null`/"unknown" vs `cost: 0`/"free") in the catalog schema — happy to follow whichever contract you prefer.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Feishu recall events are not handled — bot may reply to recalled messages or leave orphan replies.

- On pre-reply check: verify message still exists

target.title: CLI: add stable setup surface export for dynamic provider/channel configuration UIs
target.body:
# CLI: add a stable setup surface export for dynamic provider/channel configuration UIs

## Summary

This PR adds a stable, machine-readable setup surface export for OpenClaw so external UIs can render provider and channel configuration dynamically without importing internal runtime chunks or scraping interactive CLI output.

Today, setup metadata already exists across:

- plugin manifests
- provider auth choices
- channel setup wizards
- setup-only plugin entries

But OpenClaw does not yet expose a supported serialized contract for exporting that setup information.
As a result, downstream UIs either hardcode a small subset of flows or depend on brittle internal implementation details.

This PR adds a minimal, stable, read-only export seam without changing the behavior of existing `setup` or `configure` commands.

## Problem

An external configuration UI that wants to track OpenClaw's real supported surface needs to know:

- which providers and channels are available
- how they should be labeled and ordered
- whether they are installed or configured
- which fields they require
- whether setup is token-based, env-based, browser/oauth-based, polling-based, or multi-step
- which status, hints, and docs should be displayed

Current interfaces are not enough:

- `openclaw plugins list --json` is plugin inventory, not setup surface
- `openclaw configure` is interactive, not machine-readable
- importing internal `dist` chunks is unstable across builds and versions
- current setup wizards are executable JS objects, not a public JSON contract

That leaves downstream UIs stuck with two weak choices:

- hardcode provider/channel flows
- depend on internal implementation details that may break on upgrade

## Proposal

Add a dedicated setup surface export command.

Suggested form:

- `openclaw configure surface --json-out <file>`

Optional flags may include:

- `--section providers|channels`
- `--installed-only`
- `--workspace <dir>`

This command writes a complete setup surface document to a JSON file for external UIs to consume.

A key design principle is:

- machine consumers must not depend on clean stdout
- the supported protocol should be the output file

That avoids JSON corruption from mixed logs or warnings.

## Why this is low risk

This PR:

- does not change existing `openclaw setup`
- does not change existing `openclaw configure`
- does not change existing plugin setup behavior
- only adds a read-only export seam
- allows external UIs to stop depending on internal chunk exports

This is not a rewrite of configure/onboarding. It is a formal export for setup surfaces that already exist.

## Why this fits the plugin/extensibility model

This is not an app-specific special case. It formalizes OpenClaw's existing plugin-defined setup capabilities.

In other words:

- channel plugins continue to define setup via `ChannelSetupWizard` and setup entries
- provider plugins continue to define auth/setup via provider auth choices and provider metadata
- core only serializes those existing setup surfaces into stable descriptors
- any UI can consume the same export, not just one client

This strengthens the plugin architecture and makes plugin-defined setup surfaces reusable.

## Suggested exported content

For each provider or channel, export:

- `id`
- `kind`
  - `provider` or `channel`
- `label`
- `detailLabel`
- `selectionLabel`
- `blurb`
- `docsPath`
- `systemImage` when available
- `installed`
- `configured`
- `selectionHint`
- `statusLines`
- `quickstartScore`
- `fields`
  - serializable field descriptors derived from credentials, text inputs, env shortcuts, etc.
- `actions`
  - serializable action descriptors such as submit, open browser, poll, install plugin, etc.
- `capabilities`
  - for example multi-account, oauth, polling, env shortcut, gateway-required

The focus of this PR is exporting setup surface descriptors, not rewriting setup execution semantics.

## Non-goals

This PR does not attempt to:

- replace existing interactive `configure`
- redesign setup wizard internals
- expose setup wizard JS objects directly as public API
- expose unstable internal runtime module layouts
- add app-specific behavior for a single client

## Implementation approach

Minimal implementation path:

1. Define serializable setup surface descriptor types.
2. Implement an exporter that builds descriptors from existing provider/channel setup metadata.
3. Add a `configure surface` command.
4. Support `--json-out`.
5. Keep logs on stderr and let machine consumers rely only on the output file.

## Why `--json-out` instead of stdout-only JSON

In some environments, stdout may include logs, warnings, or diagnostics.
If the protocol depends on perfectly clean stdout, integrations become brittle.

File output is a better long-term machine interface because it avoids:

- stdout contamination
- JSON extraction/cleanup heuristics
- inconsistent behavior across environments

For that reason, this PR proposes:

- `--json-out <file>`

as the supported machine interface.

## Value for downstream UIs

With this export, external UIs can:

- render all providers dynamically
- render all channels dynamically
- stop maintaining hardcoded setup lists
- track OpenClaw's supported surface across upgrades

This is especially useful for onboarding flows and settings dashboards.

## Compatibility

- existing CLI usage remains unchanged
- existing plugins do not need immediate changes
- older clients can ignore the new command completely
- newer clients can depend on the formal seam instead of internal implementation details

## Why this is better than client-side workarounds

Without this seam, downstream clients are forced to choose between:

- hardcoded setup UIs
- importing internal chunks
- parsing partial plugin inventory
- scraping interactive CLI output

All of these are weaker contracts than adding a small, explicit export interface.

## Possible follow-ups

This PR is intentionally the smallest useful step. Future work can build on it with:

- richer provider setup surface export
- setup session execution APIs
- gateway/RPC access to setup surfaces
- more formal plugin-sdk public types

But this PR itself stays intentionally narrow:

- a stable, read-only, machine-consumable setup surface export

target.title: feat(routing): support per-binding workspace override
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds an optional `workspace` field to route bindings so that the same agent can operate in different directories depending on which chat/peer it responds to.
- Threads the workspace through `ResolvedAgentRoute` so downstream consumers can use it.
- In `getReplyFromConfig`, binding workspace takes precedence over the agent-level default workspace.

### Motivation

In git-worktree workflows, each worktree lives in a separate directory but logically belongs to the same agent. Currently, the only way to point an agent at a different directory is to create a separate agent entry per workspace. With per-binding workspace, a single agent definition can serve multiple worktrees — each bound to a different chat/group via the existing binding mechanism.

**Example config:**
```json
{
  "bindings": [{
    "agentId": "coder",
    "workspace": "/path/to/feature-worktree-A",
    "match": { "channel": "slack", "peer": { "kind": "group", "id": "C_CHANNEL_A" } }
  }, {
    "agentId": "coder",
    "workspace": "/path/to/feature-worktree-B",
    "match": { "channel": "slack", "peer": { "kind": "group", "id": "C_CHANNEL_B" } }
  }]
}
```

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.agents.ts` | Add `workspace?: string` to `AgentRouteBinding` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts` | Add `workspace` to `RouteBindingSchema` |
| `src/routing/resolve-route.ts` | Thread `workspace` through `ResolvedAgentRoute` and `choose()` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts` | Add `resolveBindingWorkspace()` — binding workspace takes precedence |

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify existing routing tests pass (`pnpm test -- --grep routing`)
- [ ] Add a binding with `workspace` field and confirm the agent runs in that directory
- [ ] Confirm bindings without `workspace` behave identically to before (no regression)
- [ ] Verify `resolveBindingWorkspace` returns `undefined` when no binding matches

target.title: fix(agent-job): surface pending error snapshot when outer timeout fires
target.body:
### What

Fixes a race condition in `waitForAgentJob` that caused pending agent errors to be discarded when the primary timeout fired.

### Why

When an agent job fails, an `error` event schedules the job to finish with an error snapshot after a 15-second grace period. If the outer `timeoutMs` for `waitForAgentJob` fires before this grace period elapses, it calls `finish(null)`, canceling the pending error.

The caller receives a generic timeout message instead of the actual root cause, making debugging difficult.

### How

The outer timeout callback now checks for a pending error via `getPendingAgentRunError()` before resolving with `null`. This function reads from a module-level map that is populated as soon as the `error` event is received, guaranteeing that the snapshot is available if the race occurs.

A new regression test using fake timers (`5s < 15s`) confirms the fix.

Closes #52497
target.author: martingarramon
